text: The robotic-assisted radical completion cholecystectomy begins with the surgeon mobilizing the duodenum, preparing to dissect to the right of the common bile duct, posterior to the duodenum. The dissection starts at the inferior border of the porta hepatis to achieve a complete lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon skeletonizes the arterial structures, beginning dissection at the common hepatic artery. Lymphatic tissues are dissected towards the liver, and the peritoneal layer of the porta hepatis is divided cephalad. The common hepatic artery is skeletonized, revealing the origin of the gastroduodenal artery. The bile duct is identified using the FireFly system after an IV endocyanin green injection, causing the biliary system to fluoresce. Dissection continues to clear the common bile duct medially, along the proper hepatic artery, with lymphatic tissue dissected upwards towards the liver base. Tissue between the bile duct and hepatic artery is dissected and removed, exposing the anterior aspect of the portal vein.

The surgeon removes lymphatic tissue lateral and posterior to the porta hepatis, retracting the bile duct medially to clear posterior tissue while being mindful of the portal vein. An accessory branch of the cystic artery is identified and divided. The lymphatic tissue between the bile duct and hepatic artery is removed, and the bile duct is skeletonized laterally. The right hepatic artery is identified coursing posterior to the bile duct, visible as it enters the right hilus of the liver. The anterior aspect of the portal vein is visualized, along with the common bile duct, portal vein, common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, gastroduodenal artery, and cystic artery.

The surgeon uses the Firefly system to confirm the cystic duct stump, then divides the cystic artery and excises the cystic duct stump. The stump is sent for frozen section evaluation, and the entry of the cystic duct stump into the bile duct is over-sewn.

The surgeon places the specimens in an endocatch bag for retrieval from the abdomen. The liver resection begins with marking the transection plane for a two-centimeter resection of segments 4B and 5. Near the hilum, the surgeon carefully preserves vascular structures, including the right hepatic artery. Parenchymal transection is performed superficially using cautery, followed by a bipolar vessel sealing device with a saline drip to prevent char buildup. The laparoscopic ultrasonic dissector is used for precise dissection, preserving vessels. Small vessels are divided with the bipolar sealing device, while larger vessels are clipped.
The specimen is placed in an endocatch bag and removed from the surgical field. Hemostasis is achieved, and the final appearance of the porta hepatis is displayed, showing the completed dissection.
